observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, the Sangguniang Bayan, and the members of the Finance Committee:

Review and revise the income forecasting methods and ensure that income projections are based on realistic assumptions and historical trends.

Establish clear monitoring procedures for the inflows of revenue and flows of cash to regularly assess the realization of projected income and to adjust accordingly any unrealized revenue.

Henceforth, enhance the cash management practices including establishing contingency plans to address potential cash shortfall and employ austerity measures on some of its non-essential activities so as to recover from the cash overdraft incurred in CY 2023.
observations.managementAction:
Temporarily refrained any spending or financial commitments based on existing income projections until the actual income realized and/or available funds are determined.

Gathered historical financial data to provide a more realistic assumption of income trends and patterns.

Projected income estimates based on actual historical performance data, set a buffer for unexpected fluctuations, and limit assumptions that may lead to inflated estimates.
observations.reason:
Lack of personnel with technical expertise on cash management and budgeting.

Incoherence of understanding of the availability of appropriation and cash.

Reiterated in Part II of this report

Revisit the Manual of Operation of the SWM Program and ensure that the minimum standards and processes prescribed in RA 9003 are complied with to ensure the safety of the SWM workers and minimize exposure to health risks of the residents/constituents within the collection vicinity.

Include in the plan and appropriation provisions for the training of the SWM workers in the proper handling of wastes which should include the importance of using the complete safety clothing. Ensure that the provision for the procurement of appropriate safety clothing and equipment for their SWM employees is included in the AIP/APP of the LGU each year considering that such supplies deteriorate within a couple of months.

Direct the Solid Waste Management Unit/MENRO to ensure that any person entering the Sanitary Landfill must wear the appropriate safety clothing to minimize health risks.

Orientation and training were conducted.

Procurement of safety gears for the SWM workers is included in the AIP/PPA of the LGU for CY 2024.

Safety gears were procured and will be released in the 4th Quarter of CY 2024.

Insufficient funds and frequent turnover of JO workers assigned in the SWM.





Lack of awareness of the JO workers on the hazards in the Sanitary Landfill and insufficient workforce to secure the vicinity and enforce the requirement.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Local Budget Officer and other officials concerned to:
Establish clear procedures in monitoring financial transactions, including regular reconciliations and reviews to identify and promptly rectify discrepancies.
Review previous years’ transactions and identify the specific PPA and/or sources of the balances under the 20% DF and effect the necessary correction in the records.
Require periodic progress reports on fund utilization and project implementation to facilitate proactive issue resolution; and
Ensure the continuity and accuracy of the data presented in the Utilization Reports.

We recommended for the Municipal Mayor to:
Require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to transfer the current year and previous years unexpended QRF and DRRMF- MOOE to the Special Trust Fund under Trust Liabilities-DRRM account at year-end in compliance to COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.
Require the Municipal Accountant to properly monitor and maintain an accurate subsidiary record of transactions and balances of the Trust Liabilities-DRRM account.
Require the Municipal Budget Officer to maintain separate registries for QRF, MOOE and CO and for each of the four thematic areas of the 70% Mitigation Fund.
Require the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Budget Officer and MDRRMO to reconcile and adjust their respective records and reports so as to respectively provide therein accurate and reliable amounts of available appropriations, their utilization, and balances at each reporting period, including the accurate balances of liability and cash accounts pertaining to DRRM Funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure proper accountability and compliance with COA Circular 97-02 on the grant and liquidation of cash advances by employing the following corrective measures:
Limit the amount and frequency of cash advances granted to a single Accountable Officer, and consider delegating functions to other qualified personnel to avoid overburdening.
Strictly require the timely preparation and submission of Reports of Disbursements, duly supported by official receipts, payrolls, and other documents;
Stop authorizing the grant of additional cash advance unless the previous advances are fully liquidated and accounted for.

To address the inefficient utilization of the GAD fund and ensure that gender-sensitive activities/programs are incorporated into LGU funded projects, we recommended the following:
Formulate a gender-responsive budgeting framework to ensure that gender considerations are incorporated at LGU implemented projects.
Establish coordination between the GAD Focal person/Office and other departments concerned to ensure GAD integration into LGU funded PPAs.
Implement a system for tracking and evaluating the GAD Fund utilization.
Use the GAD Accomplishment Report Template set by the Philippine Commission on Women.

We reiterated our recommendation in CY 2023 AAR for the Municipal Mayor, the Sangguniang Bayan and the members of the Finance Committee to:
Review and revise the income forecasting methods and ensure that income projections are based in realistic assumptions and historical trends.
Establish and implement a regular income monitoring to update and adjust accordingly the budget registries to reflect the actual income realized and to prevent incurrence of obligations without adequate funding support.
Henceforth, enhance the cash management practices including establishing contingency plans to address potential cash shortfall and employ austerity measures on some of its non-essential activities so as to recover from the cash overdraft incurred in CY 2024.
Intensify collection campaign from the LGUs’ identified source of income.

We recommended that the prescribed timelines for processing and payment of progress billing be strictly adhered to. Also, we recommend that the LGU establish a billing tracking and monitoring system that flags delays and identifies bottlenecks. Further, require the offices concerned to document and justify any delay in processing and payment.

To address the issues identified we recommended the following courses of action to the Local chief Executive:
Require the finance committee, particularly the Accountant, Budget Officer and Treasurer, to certify the availability of funds before entering into contract and reserve such appropriation for the payment of service to ensure availability of sufficient fund throughout the contract duration.
Require the Budget Officer and the Accountant to employ stringent budget monitoring to ensure that appropriations match the contracted services.
Require the HRMO and the Treasurer to ensure the timely processing and payment of compensation to JO Employees.

To address these concerns and ensure that hiring practices adhere to the principles of transparency, efficiency, and necessity, we recommended that the LCE limit the hiring of Job Order workers only to indispensable functions and pay the daily minimum wage prescribed by the DBM-CSC-COA JC No. 1, series of 2017 by employing the following measures:
Require the HRMO to determine the necessity of hiring, and assess the duties and functions of JO workers, taking into account their expertise, experience, and capability to handle the position to come up with the ideal number of personnel to be hired;
Require the MPDC and HRMO to align JO hiring with actual workload demands, ensuring that personnel are engaged only where there is a legitimate need. Also, to develop a clear and equitable wage structure for JO personnel to prevent inconsistencies and unjust compensation practices.
Require all departments to submit detailed justifications for JO hiring, including workload analysis and impact assessments.
Provide in the contract of service the specific details or information such as expected output/service to be rendered, duration of work, office/project assignment, source of funds, and its daily rate; and
Require each JO worker to prepare the accomplishment report, stating therein the actual and specific tasks undertaken during the period, duly verified and accepted by the immediate supervisor.

We recommended that the provision for the HDMF contribution share of the LGU be appropriately provided in the appropriation of the General and Special Education Fund for the JO Employees mandated to be enrolled in the HDMF Membership. Henceforth, ensure that the LGU remit the required contribution including the LGU share to the HDMF.

We recommended for the LCE the following corrective measures to comply with the statutory and mandatory requirements of HDMF:
Require the HR and Finance Departments to ensure that employees’ wages are paid on time to facilitate the timely deduction and remittance of HDMF contributions.
Designate a specific department/employee to prepare the HDMF remittance documentation and maintain its records to ensure that all withheld contributions are remitted on time. It should be considered that the liquidation documents are usually submitted to the Accounting Office beyond the deadline for remittance, thus, requiring such an office to prepare the remittance documents would only lead to delayed remittances as well.
Require the Accounting Department to update the HDMF Contributions records and reconcile all withheld HDMF contributions and remit the amounts immediately to avoid further penalties and interest.
Designate a temporary Liaison to address and resolve any discrepancies in contributions and remittances with HDMF.

We recommended that the LCE take immediate measures to ensure procurement of comprehensive insurance coverage for all insurable properties of the LGU, including the vehicles, infrastructure and other valuable properties. The insurance coverage should be for fire, theft, natural disasters and other relevant coverage based on the specific risks faced by the LGU.
Likewise, we recommended that the Inventory Committee review the existing Inventory List of PPE and identify those that are insurable and would require insurance coverage. Ensure that the required PIF is appropriately filled out, updated and accurate, and submitted to COA and GSIS to facilitate proper insurance documentation.

We recommended that the Local School Board observe the fund allocation and utilization reporting, transparency, and monitoring by employing the following suggested actions:
Enhance the implementation of the information dissemination on how the SEF is allocated and utilized. Consider the implementation of an online portal or public dashboard or any social media resources available in the locality to ensure greater visibility and/or make the allocation and utilization report accessible to stakeholders.
Require the Accountant to render detailed quarterly and annual reports on the specific use of the SEF which should include the amount spent on particular programs/activities provided and the outcomes achieved.
Mandate the official/committee concerned to submit comprehensive post-activity reports immediately upon the completion of the funded program/activity. The report should include the details of the challenges faced, measurable results, and outcome evaluation metrics to assess the direct impact of the program/activity.
Implement a monitoring and feedback scheme that allows for ongoing tracking of the implementation and outcomes of the SEF-funded projects and timely adjustments, if necessary. This should include regular progress reports which are updated in real-time or at frequent intervals.

We recommended that the Local School Board be actively involved in the SEF budget preparation process by ensuring that the budget preparation timelines are communicated on time with all the members of the LSB to provide them ample time to review and provide input on the proposed budget.
Furthermore, facilitate regular consultations and forums with key stakeholders, particularly the teachers, parents and students to encourage inputs in the identification of the programmed activities/projects to ensure that the SEF budget aligns with the educational needs of the different Districts.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following to the Solid Waste Management Unit:

Install physical security and a secure gate at the entrance to minimize unauthorized visitors and personnel in the facility. This will also address health and safety issues by imposing limitations on access.

Inspect the trucks entering the facility to confirm the waste to be disposed of then direct the vehicle to the designated area depending on the identified waste. This process will compensate for the non-segregation of waste from the source and will ensure that only residual waste is dumped in the landfill.

The daily logs of the wastes dumped appropriately categorized should be maintained. The receipt slip/record should identify the vehicle dimension and waste volume estimate to provide a reasonable basis of the recorded wastes dumped in the SLF.

Likewise, we recommended that the Solid Waste Management Board ensure that monitoring and implementing roles performed on the solid waste management program should be accordingly delineated and independent from each other in order to improve the reporting and evaluation framework for the SWM operation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Verify and adjust all book reconciling items found/noted to reflect the correct balance of Cash in Bank accounts in the financial statements as at year end.
Prepare and submit monthly Bank Reconciliation Statement duly supported with subsidiary ledgers and bank statements for all the accounts maintained by the Municipality.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare a Journal Entry Voucher to reclassify completed infrastructure projects from CIP to the appropriate Infrastructure Asset account and provide the corresponding depreciation thereof. Also, direct the Engineering Office and Accounting Office to coordinate on the monitoring of project status to make the appropriate reclassification of completed projects on time.

Further, require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that such and all other financial transactions are recorded in accordance with the pertinent accounting guidelines to present fairly the Financial Statements of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory Committee to conduct regular inventory, inspection and assessment of all the properties of the LGU which should include the identification of all unserviceable PPE and proposal of the appropriate mode of disposal. Also, require the Municipal Accountant to disclose accordingly the actual conditions of all unserviceable properties in the Notes to FS.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entry, and ensure to recognize only as Accounts Payable the goods and services already delivered or billed by the suppliers/contractors, as warranted by the Delivery Receipts and other appropriate supporting documents duly inspected and accepted by the management.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to take up necessary adjusting/correcting entries in the books on the advance payments of RPT and SET collected in CY 2023. Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant should record advance payments of Basic RPT and SET in their appropriate books.

We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Budget Officer and MDRRM Officer to reconcile and adjust their respective records and reports so as to respectively provide therein accurate and reliable amounts of available appropriations, their utilization, and balances at each reporting period, including the accurate balances of liability and cash accounts pertaining to DRRM funds.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Engineer to update the tarpaulin signboards in compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2013-004 and include the pictures of the same as supporting documents in the payment of progress billings of infrastructure projects.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to verify thoroughly and review the supporting documents of claims for travel to ensure that only those with valid and proper authority are allowed. We further recommended to stop the practice of hiring public utility vehicle during official travels, instead, opt for the most economical and efficient mode of transportation. Otherwise, set a clear fare limits on the use of specially hired vehicle and other option when it is deemed justifiable and necessary under prevailing circumstances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, the Sangguniang Bayan and the members of the Finance Committee:
Review and revise the income forecasting methods and ensure that income projections are based in realistic assumptions and historical trends.
Establish clear monitoring procedures of the inflows of revenue and flows of cash to regularly assess the realization of projected income and to adjust accordingly any unrealized revenue.
Henceforth, enhance the cash management practices including establishing contingency plans to address potential cash shortfall and employ austerity measures on some of its non-essential activities so as to recover from the cash overdraft incurred in CY 2023.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, the Sangguniang Bayan and the members of the Finance Committee:
Establish clear procedures in monitoring financial transactions, including regular reconciliations and reviews to identify and promptly rectify discrepancies.
Review previous years’ transactions and identify the specific purpose and/or sources of the balances under the 20% DF and effect the necessary correction in the records.
Ensure the continuity of the information/data presented in the Utilization and/or Liquidation Reports.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer and other officials concerned to initiate civil remedies for the collection of rental fees and surcharges either by administrative or judicial action in compliance with the Catarman Revised Omnibus Revenue Code of 2012, other Codes, and applicable laws and regulations.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer strictly impose the payment deadlines and appropriate sanctions/ penalties on lessees upon non-payment of rental fees in accordance with the existing contracts of lease and applicable Revenue Code.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, Sanggunian Bayan, Municipal Treasurer and other officials/employees concerned coordinate with each other to formulate strategies to improve collections of RPT and SET.  Likewise, we recommend that management avail all of the legal remedies provided under RA 7160 to collect all taxes due to the LGU, in coordination with the Provincial Government.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to (1) verify the validity of payables aged two (2) years and more and send confirmation letters for balances with identified claimants/creditors, otherwise, cause the reversion of the payables to the unappropriated surplus; (2) exert efforts to retrieve the documents of unidentified payables or those without details to properly classify the amounts recorded; and (3) furnish or inform the Auditor’s Office for monitoring purposes.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Chief of Police of PNP Catarman to exhaust all remedies to locate the whereabouts of the missing firearms and return these, together with the existent firearm, to the LGU for proper sealing.  Likewise, redeposit these firearms to PNP and ensure that these will be stored in a safe location and restricted from any unauthorized use.

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to always ensure that these firearms are safely kept in the premises of the PNP Catarman and prepare an Inventory Custodian Slip issued to the end-user and appropriately record in the stock cards to establish accountability and monitor its whereabouts.

Municipal Accountant to record all inventory items acquired in its related inventory account.  Also, maintain the required Supplies Ledger Cards to monitor the receipts and issuances of supplies.

adequately be trained to discharge effective custodial function and
maintain the required stock cards.

c. In the meantime, require the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to
reconcile their records and furnish the inventory committee a copy
thereof to assist the committee in the conduct of the physical count of
the PPE.

d. Also, require the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to identify the
unserviceable and obsolete properties so that proper disposal of the
properties can be done. Pending the appropriate disposal, require the
Accountant to accordingly disclose the actual condition in the Notes to
FS.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal
Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to coordinate the recorded
obligation and identify the continuing appropriations for inclusion in the
Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts.

We recommended the following courses of actions to the Local Chief
Executive:

a. Require the Municipal Treasurer to verify the reports submitted by the
RCC with the Official Receipts used, the logbook for the released AF
to the RCC and conduct the physical count of the AF in the hands of
the collectors at least every turn over and/or end of the month so as
to ensure that all collections are turned over and completely reported
in the RCD.

b. Require the Municipal Accountant to verify the report submitted by
the Municipal Treasurer with the supporting documents and the
reported AF used so as to ensure that all collections and revenues are
completely recorded.

c. Require both the Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to
reconcile their records to ensure the accuracy and fair presentation of
the Financial Reports.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal
Treasurer to direct all collecting officers to regularly report and remit all
their collections daily thru the preparation of the Report of Collections and
Deposits at the close of each business day.

Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer be required to
deposit the collections and any LGU funds in her possession intact in the
depository bank.

We recommended that sufficient supporting documents be submitted to
ascertain the nature of the transaction. Likewise, clearly establish/state the
nature of payment in the DVs and other documents submitted considering
that the required supporting documents and rules and regulations applied
differ in each type of disbursement/expense.

Moreover, we recommended that the LCE require the municipal
accountant to record the liquidation of intelligence fund only upon receipt
of the transmittal letter duly received by the ICFAU, otherwise, require the
AO to submit the liquidation documents to validate the disbursements
made.

This audit recommendation is without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of
Suspension/Disallowance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the following officials
to do the following courses of action:

a. The Municipal Treasurer to prepare a duly certified list of taxpayers
and their respective tax due and collectible as at December 31, 2021
based on the assessment roll provided by the Assessor and furnish a copy
to the Municipal Account; and

b. The Municipal Accountant to set up RPT and SET Receivable at the
beginning of the year and adopt the modified accrual method of
accounting for RPT Income.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Economic
Enterprise Operations Officer to prepare and submit financial reports for
each of the OEE to be submitted thru the Local Finance Committee for
review and assessment of the financial sustainability of the LEE and to
decide on the continued operation or to devise options for the attainment of
the objective of the creation of the economic enterprises for the services of
its constituents and self-sufficiency of the LEE.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Local Finance
Committee to appropriately allocate the expenditures in the Annual Budget
of the LGU observing compliance on the limitations prescribed in RA 7160.
Likewise, we recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan exercise due
diligence in the review and in assuring compliance with statutory and
administrative requirements of the annual regular and supplemental
budget of the LGU.

We recommended to the Local Chief Executive the following courses of
action:

a. Require the Budget Officer to revise the Schedule of Plantilla Position
to incorporate the realignment/augmentation of the fund.

b. Submit an Appropriation Ordinance authorizing the augmentation of
the budget for Personnel Services.

c. Henceforth, instruct the Budget Officer and HRMO to incorporate the
intended salary increase in the proposed Annual Budget for each year.
This is in consideration on the requirement that the computation of the
55% PS Cap is based on the appropriation for the plantilla positions and
not the actual disbursement.

d. The realignment/augmentation of funds should specifically identify the
source of funds/savings and the items to be augmented/supplemented.

The audit recommendations are without prejudice to the issuance of
disallowance for any payment in excess of the PS Cap and authority of the
Budget Ordinance.

We recommended that the management refrain from granting fixed
monthly allowance to the members of KC-NCDDP Municipal Inter-Agency
Committee.

This recommendation is without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of
Disallowance.

We recommended that the Local chief Executive instruct the Budget Officer
and Municipal Accountant to ensure that all charges to appropriation are
with authority from the Sangguniang Bayan and/or specifically included in
the approved budget. Henceforth, ensure that any benefits paid are
authorized by existing law and regulation.

This audit recommendation is without prejudice to the issuance of a Notice
of Disallowance.

We recommended to the Local Chief Executive the following:

a. Require the HRMO to prepare a detailed and specific plan of activities
per Department integrating the volume and frequency of regular and
routine work to be performed and the expected output and deadline;

b. In issuing the authority to render overtime services, ensure that the
expected output, date of completion/submission of the report, and the
authorized compensation is specifically provided;

c. Consider the provision on the compensatory time/day-off, in lieu of the
overtime pay or non-compensatory remuneration policy for overtime
services pursuant to CSC and DBM JC No. 2;

d. Require the concerned officials to submit the DTR and a detailed
accomplishment during the regular work hours, and accomplishment
report and overtime work plan in all the overtime compensation
claimed; and

e. Refrain from granting overtime services with pay to Job Order
employees.

These recommendations are without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of
Suspension/Disallowance as a result of our audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor install control and monitoring
mechanisms to ensure that the use of government vehicles and consumption
of fuel shall strictly be for a valid public purpose. Henceforth, the following
documents and reports should be required on the sue of government
vehicles and fuel consumption:

a) Serially numbered Driver’s Trip Tickets approved by the Municipal
Mayor or his authorized representative to control the use of government
vehicle;

b) Monthly Report of Official Travels prepared by the driver concerned
and Monthly Equipment Utilization Report prepared by the equipment
custodian summarizing in chronological order his trips or use of
equipment, respectively, for the month, duly certified by the driver and
approved by the Administrative Officer; and

c) Report of Fuel Consumption shall be prepared and submitted monthly
to the auditor for verification purposes. The report shall show the
different types of motor vehicles utilized by the agency during the
month, total distance traveled, total fuel consumed and the normal fuel
consumption for each vehicle.

We recommended that the LCE:

1. Require the MSWDO, LDRRMO, MHO and other concerned offices to
provide the designated Supply Officer of the copies of the
RIS/Acknowledgment Receipt and Issuance Form for in-kind
donations; and

2. Require the designated Supply officer to maintain a record of the
receipt, distribution and balances of in-kind donations and prepare the
required reports/summary.

We recommended for the Municipal Mayor to:

a. Require the contractors to post the warranty security after the issuance
of the Certificate of Final Acceptance. To ensure that the warranty
security is posted, the LGU may make it a requirement to observe the
one (1) year period after completion of the project before returning the
retention money after which the warranty period shall commence.

b. Observe the period requirement provided in Section 62.2.3.2 in the
imposition of the warranty security which is 2 to 15 years after final
acceptance depending on the nature of the infrastructure project.

c. Require the BAC to maintain a record and monitor the validity of
posted securities so that subsequent replacement thereof upon
expiration can be demanded immediately.

We recommended the following courses of action to the Local Chief
Executive to address the minimal collection of RPT:

a) Request from the Finance Committee and Sangguniang Bayan to
include in the appropriation the upgrading of the current system of
assessment record.

b) Require the Assessor’s Office to adopt a systematic method of filing and
recommend an updated systematic method of the assessment record.

c) Require the Municipal Assessor to update the real property assessment
roll to establish a realistic and accurate RPT receivable.

d) Further, require the Municipal Assessor and Treasurer to reconcile
their record, especially, the identification information on each of the real
property within the municipality for easy referencing and retrieval of
records.

e) Require the Municipal Treasurer to conduct intensive tax campaign and
information drive in order to invite more taxpayers to pay taxes.

f) Enforce collection of delinquent real property taxes by conducting tax
campaign and offering incentives of full payment of tax dues.

g) Further, impose the remedies provided for and exercise its taxing
authority mandated under the Local Government Code of 1991.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:

a. the MPDC and Finance Committee to submit the Local Development
and Financial Plan specifying therein the specific activities and expected
results of the programs to ensure that the use of funds will directly
benefit the public, and

b. The HRMO to prepare the manning schedule with information of the
work assignments and functions of every Job Order employees to
prevent duplication of task between them.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to ensure that all the properties
of the LGU are properly maintained, managed and safeguarded against loss
and wastage through the following courses of action:

a. Require the MPDC and Engineer to regularly monitor the
status/condition of the infrastructure projects of the LGU and
recommend appropriate actions for its maintenance and/or repairs.

b. Require the Property Custodian to maintain records of all the LGU
properties and monitor its storage. Also, identify the offices in need of
the motorcycles and accordingly issue so as to put the equipment into
use.

c. Require the Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count of the
PPE owned by the LGU and prepare a report thereon to be submitted
to the Office of the Auditor;

d. Create an Appraisal Committee to facilitate the immediate disposal of
the unserviceable properties in order to prevent further deterioration
and loss of recoverable value.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal
Treasurer to examine the conditions of these vehicles and equipment,
determine the most advantageous actions to the municipality, and give
attention to Section 79 of P.D. No. 1445, if found to be no longer serviceable
or needed by the municipality.

We recommended that the Management concerned institute and implement properly the accounting and control system of inventory as required in the new Government Accounting Manual for LGUs. We further recommended that Municipal Mayor direct all Department Heads to coordinate and support with the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer in the proper implementation thereof especially in the conduct of physical count of the unused/unconsumed inventories at yearend.

We recommended that the Management concerned should create a Committee in charged for the proper inventory, valuation and reporting of the Biological Assets at yearend. We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a.	Municipal Accountant to recognize gains and losses due to changes in the fair value, impairment loss if any, of the Biological Assets in the Financial Statements; and,

b.	Acting Municipal Agriculturist to submit a complete and timely reports especially the RPCBA, to the Offices of the Municipal Accountant and the Auditor, necessary to provide bases in accounting and auditing of the balance of Biological Assets, and its related accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: (a) Petty cash custodian to be directly responsible and accountable as to the receipt, disbursements and recording of the Petty Cash Fund, and render the accounts immediately upon the conduct of cash examination; (b) Municipal Accountant, as internal auditor, to facilitate the proper implementation of the petty cash fund transactions; (a) Management concerned, to purchase the safety vault or safety box for the use of Petty Cash Custodian in the safekeeping of petty cash vouchers, petty cash book and cash items.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to: (a) ensure that all AFs issued to a particular AO be recorded in the Cashbook and properly reported in the RCD; (b) continue the practice of setting aside a particular stub of OR number series for nonrevenue cash receipts transactions; and, (c) discontinue the practice of citing “Direct to Cashbook” in order to avoid confusion to what really this transaction all about in this LGU.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) in coordination with the Municipal Accountant to revisit the guidelines in reviewing the legality and veracity of the documents submitted of the prospective suppliers prior to award the contract.

observations.recommendation:
1.13	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to:
a.  	Prepare the necessary journal entries to correct the erroneous balances of PPE accounts;
b.	Reclassify the completed project to its appropriate asset account; and
c.	Be guided with the descriptions of the accounts provided under COA Circular No. 2015-009, so that the correct accounts will be used for each disbursement for progress billing, and to prevent the misstatement of each account.

2.7	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the remaining balance of the BGCM fund in the Trust Liabilities account as a Special Account in the General Fund as of December 31, 2020, and correct the Due to NGAs account in order that these accounts will be fairly presented in the Statement of Financial Position.

4.11	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and other employees concerned, to secure complete documentation on all disbursements, to ensure the validity, and propriety of the transactions.  Moreover, require the Municipal Treasurer to attach the official receipts of the refund/return of excess cash advance in the Report of Disbursement to reflect the total liquidation of the fund.

Significantly, management thru the LCE and Municipal Accountant should strictly adhere to the policy of no payment without complete documentation.
6.10	We also recommended for the submission of the lacking supporting documents for the aforementioned transactions.  This is without prejudice to the issuance of Notices of Suspension as a result of our audit.

9.12	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and municipal officials concerned to stop the grant of honoraria and incentives in so far as those undertakings that has no legal basis and are considered as an ordinary discharge of official duties and responsibilities.

11.9	We recommended that Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to immediately take appropriate actions to recover the amount from the estate of the deceased accountable officer to avoid losses of funds.

12.6	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to release to the Barangay Treasurers, on a quarterly basis, the barangay share from RPT and PCA collections in order for the barangay to utilize the fund for its operations.

13.6	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Accountant, Municipal Budget Officer, and other municipal officials concerned to act within the letter and spirit of all the rules, regulations, and directives issued by the National Government relative to CoViD-19 pandemic.

14.7	We recommended that Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to revisit the stipulations in the Memorandum of Agreement, Trust Agreement or Memorandum of Understanding between the LGU and source agencies to properly identify the required/applicable mode of disposition of the unutilized balance of funds transferred, and require them to immediately comply as stated.

15.5	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the municipal officials and employees concerned to strictly comply with the provisions of Section 93 of PD No. 1445, and Sections 10 and 48 of RA No. 9184 to ensure that the LGU obtain the most advantageous prices in its procurement of goods and services.
15.6	We further recommended that procurement through reimbursement basis be discouraged as this prevents the withholding of taxes required by existing regulations.

16.6	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor should establish clear-cut guidelines for the grant of communication allowance to foster equity in relation to the actual communication needs of the LGU officials and employees relative to their position and function.

17.9	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer, and all LGU officials concerned to comply with the provisions of RA No. 9184, COA and GPPB JMC No. 1, and COA Circular No. 2012-001 to ensure propriety and validity of the procurements, and that the LGU obtain the most advantageous price in its procurements.

18.8	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and Municipal Treasurer adopt, and process the enrollment to the ATM payroll system to lessen the risks of loss, strengthen the controls in handling cash for payroll, and improve efficiency in the treasury operations.

19.9	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the officials and employees concerned to:
a. 	Formulate internal policy on the implementation of Biometrics machine as the official register to record daily attendance of the municipal employees and personnel;
b. 	Prepare monthly report of undertime and absences based on the DTRs to monitor compliance with the CSC policies on government hours; and
c. 	Effect the updating of leave cards based thereon, to avoid the possible loss of government funds in the form of overpayment of leave benefits.

20.11	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, Municipal Engineer, and other local officials concerned to:
a. 	Re-evaluate and re-assess the programmed priority PPAs embodied in the AIPs, and review if the AIPs are in line with the Local Development Investment Program of the Municipality to ensure full utilization of the fund;
b. 	Coordinate with other local officials concerned to ensure the full implementation of the 20% DF PPAs during the budget year; and
c. 	Implement immediately all unimplemented PPAs that are in line with the Local Development Investment Program of the LGU for the attainment of socio-economic, and environmental management outcomes.

21.11	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) require all the personnel concerned, especially the Municipal Accountant, to secure complete documentation on all disbursements and the necessary data peculiar to the transaction, to ensure the validity and propriety of the transactions.
21.12	Moreover, we recommended that the LCE to exercise caution in entering into agreements and see to it that compliance with the terms and conditions would always be advantageous to the LGU and its constituents.
21.13	Furthermore, we recommended for the submission of the lacking supporting documents of the aforementioned transactions.  This is without prejudice to the issuance of Notices of Suspension as a result of our audit.

We recommended that the Management concerned institute and implement properly the accounting and control system of inventory as required in the new Government Accounting Manual for LGUs. Acting Municipal Agriculturist to submit a complete and timely reports especially the RPCBA, to the Offices of the Municipal Accountant and the Auditor, necessary to provide bases in accounting and auditing of the balance of Biological Assets, and its related accounts.

observations.recommendation: 1.11	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require all accountable officials and employees of the LGU with outstanding cash advances to immediately settle their cash advances by submitting the necessary documents as proof of the disbursements made or through the cash refund of the unused cash advances, otherwise, cause the withholding of salaries to settle their cash advances. Likewise, stop the granting of cash advance for the purchase of equipment that will circumvent the provision of RA 9184.

2.7	To correct the deficiencies noted, we recommended the following courses of action:
Conduct an investigation to know the whereabouts and what happened to the biological assets. Determine who was accountable, and if found to be remiss in his custodianship, the person should be held liable.
If these biological assets are no longer existing and the person/s liable can’t be identified, the proper accounting thereof should be made to drop the account to avoid overstating the asset account following prescribed procedures set forth by COA.

3.5	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor should require the concerned personnel/Office to submit copies of contracts on Infrastructure Projects together with complete supporting documents within five (5) working days from the date of perfection thereof pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2009-001 to facilitate the timely review thereof and sufficient time for corrective measures before the actual implementation.

4.16	We recommended that management strictly observe the pertinent provisions of RA 8291, RA 10606 and RA 9679 on the deduction and remittance of monthly contributions and loan amortizations due to GSIS, Philhealth and Pag-IBIG in order to determine the correct amount of the outstanding due them of the employees and government agency to which the contributions and loan repayments apply.
4.17	We further recommend that the Municipal Accountant should deduct the accurate/exact amount of the employee’s contribution payable by him in accordance with the schedule prescribed in the rules and regulations of the above-mentioned implementing Acts and record the government share due the GSIS, Philhealth and Pag-IBIG upon the payment of salaries.

5.8	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant to remit the shares of the 55 barangays from the 1% Real Property Tax collection of the Municipality.  Henceforth, ensure that barangay shares from RPT collections are remitted to the respective barangays within 5 days after the end of each quarter as required under Section 271(d) of RA 7160.
5.9	Likewise, remit regularly the share of the provincial government from the 1% real property tax and the share from the Special Education Fund out of the collection of the additional 1% real property taxes.

6.12	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive see to it that the Loan Agreement entered into in behalf of the Municipality with any lending institution to finance projects of the LGU should be in accordance with the Plans and Specifications and Program of Work and in line with the purpose for which the loan was obtained to avoid doubts on the purpose for which the loan was obtained.

7.8	We recommended that for Local Economic Enterprises (LEE) to be operated by the LGU, proper planning should be done and a business plan describing the details on how the LEE will be operated to make the operations realize its objectives and be financially self-sufficient and profitable, instead of leaving it idle after its completion and consumed a large amount of the LGU funds for the payment of the amortization and interest of the loan, thus defeating the purpose of creating the economic enterprise.

8.5	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to prepare and submit financial reports of the local economic enterprise to be submitted to the Local Finance Committee for review and assessment on the financial sustainability of the LEE and to decide on the continued operation or to redefine the purpose of the enterprise.
8.6	We likewise recommended that Semi-Annual Financial Self-Sufficiency Review be made to evaluate whether the economic enterprise is viable and shall be allowed to continue operations or totally stopped its operation.

9.8	In order to address the issues and concerns, we recommended that:
The Local Chief Executive should instruct the Municipal Treasurer to strictly enforce the terms and conditions of the Contract of Lease. Also, the Municipal Treasurer should impose the 20% penalties and surcharges from delinquent stallholders as mentioned in the perfected Contract of Lease.
The Municipal Treasurer to demand collection of the unsettled accounts and/or arrears from defaulting stallholders. Issue demand letters and exhaust all means to collect the rents due.
Revenue Collecting Clerks assigned in the recording and reporting of the rental fees should see to it that the collections be properly and timely recorded in the ledgers as to recognize the income during the period it was earned.
Reconciliation should be made periodically to come up with the correct recorded income.

10.11	To correct the deficiencies, we recommended the following courses of action:
To attach to the payroll for the payment of the monthly wages of contractual and job order employees, the Daily Time Record and Accomplishment Reports.  For first payment, a copy of the contract, certification of the LCE/Personnel Officer that the activities/services `cannot be provided by regular or permanent personnel of the agency and certification by the Local Chief Executive (LCE), in case of LGUs, that the employment/hiring is still within the Personal Services (PS) limitation prescribed under Section 325(a) of RA 7160.
To provide in the contract of service the expected outputs for each job order employees hired.
To assess its existing organizational structure so that the ideal number of contractual/job order personnel shall be established.
To stop issuing a job order contract which allow them to be paid during Saturdays and Sundays when the LGU is closed and therefore there is no services rendered.

11.6	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant obligate all unpaid expenditures at end of the year to ensure that these are recorded in the period they are incurred in compliance with the provision of Section 4 of PD 1445. We further recommend that a cut-off date for the submission of unpaid claims prior to the close of the fiscal year be established to allow the obligation and recording of expenses during the year.

12.11	We recommended that the agency utilize the 20% Development Fund in accordance with the implementing rules and regulations set forth in DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 where it should be only appropriated for programs/projects which partake the nature of investment and capital expenditures in order to achieve the desirable socio-economic and environmental management outcomes.  Moreover, stop using the fund for PPAs not allowed in the circular.

13.10	We recommended that the LDRRM Fund be utilized in accordance with the guidelines provided for under NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No.  2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 for the full attainment of the objectives of the fund in the prevention, mitigation and response to calamities.
13.11	We also recommended the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entries to book up the unexpended balance of the LDRRM Fund to a special trust fund under Trust Liabilities-DRRMF account instead of under General Fund.

14.7	We recommended that the GAD Focal Person should submit, in addition to the Annual GAD Accomplishment Report, the documents required under Section 5.2 of the DBM/NEDA/NCRFW Joint Circular No. 2004-1 to facilitate validations of the accomplishments reported to determine compliance on the programs/projects/activities as provided for the General Appropriation Act.  Moreover, we recommend that the accomplishment report should indicate the specific GAD related activities to facilitate validation of the reported accomplishment.

15.10	We recommended that management should fast track the implementation of the physical facilities of the dumpsite such as the construction of the sanitary landfill and the final closure of the residual containment area of the existing dumpsite. Similarly, the Municipal Ecology Park is likewise recommended to be funded and established to attain the purpose of promoting a quality life and maintaining an ecological balance.

16.4	It is recommended that the Accountant should have the JEV approved before recording the journal entries of the transactions made by the LGU in the books of accounts and submit these to the auditor thereafter.  Likewise, it is recommended that the person who prepares the JEV should indicate and sign his name to provide information on the person who made the entry and determine responsibility on the entries made.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer to fast track the preparation of the documentary requirements including the Program of Works to facilitate commencement of the project implementation of the priority development projects and avoid incurrence of further delay.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct all concerned personnel to expedite timely implementation of the programs, projects and activities embodied in the LDRRMF Investment Plan in order to prevent funds from being idle and to continually enhance the disaster preparedness and response capability.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the LDRRMO and Municipal Budget Officer to recommend to the LDRRMC the creation of at least one (1) permanent position to be in-charge with Research and Planning; Administration and Training as well as Operations and Warning sections of the Municipal DRRM Office for adoption and approval by the Sangguniang Bayan pursuant to the aforementioned Circular.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the LSB to strictly follow the documentary and procedural requirements in the preparation of SEF budget in order to come up with a more strategic and efficient utilization of resources for priority programs and projects in compliance with the above-mentioned Joint Circular.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the LSB to coordinate with DepEd ROX for the issuance of the requirements, specifications, policy and guidelines in order to ensure strict compliance by the LSB and conformity of items procured with DepEd standards.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct all concerned personnel to discontinue the practice of matching the employees' share of Pag-IBIG contribution that is more than what is required by the aforementioned law, to avoid said unnecessary expenditure that may deplete the local funds which may result to audit disallowance.

We recommended that Management evaluate the present policy, if any, for the determination of wage rate of its Job Order personnel, otherwise, establish appropriate guidelines to ensure that wage rate is in accordance with the minimum standards set forth by government regulating bodies to avoid unfair labor practices.

We reiterate our last year’s recommendation that management require the Municipal Accountant to prepare and submit timely the Bank Reconciliation Statements on all depository accounts to comply with the provision of Section 74 of Presidential Decree No. 1445 or the Government Auditing Code of the Philippines, DBM, DOF and COA Joint Circular No. 4-86 dated January 2, 1988 and COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996 validate the accuracy of the Cash in Bank, Local Currency-Current Account shown in the financial statements.

We recommend the following courses of actions to facilitate the recording of the 38 lots in the books of accounts and establish ownership:

1.The Local Chief of Executive should instruct the Municipal Treasurer to retrieve all the Deed of Sale or Deed of Donation or any document that will establish the ownership from Offices such as the Register of Deeds, Department of Environment and Natural Resources and other Offices 
2.The Municipal Accountant should record in the books all the Lots based on the Tax Declaration on file with the Municipal Assessor at fair market value
3.As mandated in Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386, the Local Chief Executive shall ensure that all the lots should be registered under the Torrens Title System and safeguarded from squatters, unlawful occupants and the like.

We recommended that Management measure and record the changes of its biological assets and to recognize any gain or loss, and properly disclose in the notes the details as required by PPSAS 27 - Agriculture.
We recommended that the projects and programs charged to the 20% Development Fund should fall under the projects mentioned in the aforementioned Joint Memorandum Circular.

We recommended that Management maximize the unexpended portion of the prior years' LDRRMF through its inclusion in the investment plan in order to further strengthen the Municipality's capacity and equipage against possible disasters.
We recommended that that ECCD programs be given allocation taken from SEF.  Also, responsibilities provided under RA No. 10410 and Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 shall be observed.

We recommended that the LCPC be re-organized in accordance with the pertinent provision of RA No. 9344 and post in the LGU premises its organizational structure for the information of the affected and interested parties/constituents.
We recommended further that the Secretariat Head shall keep copies of minutes of minutes along with the duly signed attendance sheets.

We recommended that the minimum net take home pay requirement be complied with so as not to reduce the employees' drive towards work.  Moreover, for those loan covered with Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) between the bank or lending institution and the Local Government of Catarman, no new loan availment shall be allowed if the resulting net take home pay falls below the threshold.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant compute the  depreciation of property, plant and equipment in accordance with the procedures provided under COA Circular No. 2003-007 dated December 11, 2003 which requires the setting up of the ten percent (10%) residual value to prevent dropping of the particular property, plant and equipment from the books of the agency when it is beyond its estimated useful life.  

We further recommend that the Municipal Accountant restore the property, plant and equipment dropped from the books of accounts at its residual value to safeguard these properties from pilferage and misuse.  For unserviceable properties it should follow the procedure in the disposal of unserviceable properties as provided for in COA Circular No. 89-296 dated January 27, 1989.
observations.managementAction: Resolve the matter by following the audit recommendation

observations.recommendation: We recommend that all cash advances comply with the documentary requirements prescribed in COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 and should be granted only to bonded officials and employees in accordance with the provisions of Section 89 and 101 of PD No. 1445 the Government Auditing Code of the Philippines and COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We recommend that management stop utilizing the 20% development fund for administrative expenses and other expenses which do not partake the nature of an investment and capital expenditures and those that are not related to  and/or not connected with the implementation of development projects, programs and activities as expressly provided in DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011 to achieve optimal  socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes.

We recommend that management direct the Municipal Environment and Natural Resources Officer to expedite the completion and approval of the 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan in compliance with Republic Act 9003.  Further, require the Local Solid Waste Management Board to regularly review the plan and monitor its implementation.

We recommend that the Local School Board allocate the Special Education Fund to program/project/activity specifically enumerated in Section 271 and 100 of Republic Act 7160 and Section 7 (b) of RA No. 10410 to efficiently utilize the funds derived from the share on the proceeds of the additional 1% real property tax.  Moreover, strict compliance to the reportorial requirements should be made in accordance with the requirement of DECS-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 01-A dated March 14, 2000.

We recommend that management meticulously and judiciously plan the procurement process of the agency and prepare an Annual Procurement Plan (APP) in compliance with Rule II, Section 7 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations  of Republic Act 9184.  Moreover, the preparation of the Project Procurement  Management Plans (PPMPs) by the end-user should be required to be able to come up with an Annual Procurement Plan that will meet the need of all the units of the agency in the pursuit of its mandate.

We recommend that management require the concerned officials to submit within five (5) days after the perfection of the contract a copy of the contract and each of all the supporting documents forming part of the contract to the Auditor of the agency pursuant to COA Circular N0. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.  Likewise, copy of the Memorandum of Agreement/ Memorandum of Understanding (MOA/MOU) of all projects funded by other agencies be furnished to the Auditor to facilitate review of proper implementation of the project.  

We further recommend that management likewise submit copies of perfected contracts and each of all supporting documents of other infrastructure bid and awarded.

We recommend that management prepare and submit to the auditor the AAPSI within 60days from receipt of the audit report copy furnished the agencies concerned pursuant to the provision of Section 93 of RA 10651 to ensure that all recommendations are implemented and to provide enough time for the auditor to validate the same.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Planning and Development Officer to coordinate closely in monitoring the implementation of projects funded from 20% Development Fund.

We recommended that Management submit justification on the deviations made from the original approved itinerary of travel to the actual travel made and the submission of Certificate of Appearance from the place or identified.

We recommended that Management require the BAC to update themselves with the recent GPPB rulings as frequently as possible so as not to put the bids at risk, considering that procurement plays a crucial role on the growth of an agency because this initiative not only benefits the agency but also the community as a whole.

We recommended that Management instruct the Bids and Awards Committee to practice and comprehend the significance of integrity and transparency during the bidding process since the process itself relies on the basic principles of trust and confidence. Furthermore, we recommended that the BAC should be more mindful of the fact that confidential matters should not be discuss during the stages of bidding. We recommended that Management strictly observe the guidelines with regard the proper implementation of benchmarking activities.

We recommended that Management update its list of unserviceable properties by considering the results of the annual physical inventory and strictly observe the guidelines in the disposal of unserviceable equipment and other property in accordance with Title V – “Disposal of Supplies or Property” of the Supply and Property Management Manual for local government units.

We recommended that Management use property tag/stickers to facilitate the easy identification of government property and to ensure its proper usage. The tags/stickers should be attached to the property upon inventory-taking and to newly-acquired property and equipment before these are distributed to the end-users.

We recommended that Management strictly follow the rules on proper disposal/divestment of assets so as to optimally take advantage of the probable proceeds from sale thereof in compliance with COA Circular No. 89-296.

We recommended that Management exhaust all possible means to secure the TCTs/OCTs of its properties as soon as possible in accordance to PD No. 1445. Furthermore, the Management should bear in mind that tax declarations are not valid proof of ownership.

We recommended that Management mark all of their vehicles with “FOR OFFICIAL USE ONLY,” together with the corresponding name of the agency operating/using the motor vehicles, in accordance with the instructions of COA Circular No. 77-61, in order to avoid disallowances as instructed in COA Circular No. 75-6. 

We also recommended that Management instruct the Supply Officer and designated Driver to prepare diligently the required Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption and Monthly Report of Official Travels, respectively, as required under the existing rules and regulation. Control and monitoring of fuel consumption should be made in order to ascertain the validity and propriety on the expenses incurred on fuel, oil and gasoline and to minimize wasteful, excessive and unnecessary expenditures of fuel consumption.
